title: What To Do After a Motorcycle Accident
metaTitle: What To Do After a Motorcycle Accident — Step-by-Step
metaDescription: Motorcycle accident victims face unique challenges including bias and severe injuries. Learn the critical steps to protect your health and your claim.
description: Motorcycle accidents produce some of the most severe injuries of any road accident — and riders often face an unfair bias from insurers and juries who assume they were riding recklessly. Knowing what to do immediately, how to document your injuries, and how to counter that bias is critical to protecting your options after a crash.

sections.heading: Assess Injuries and Call for Help Immediately
sections.content: Motorcycle riders are far more vulnerable than occupants of enclosed vehicles — even a low-speed crash can result in road rash, fractures, traumatic brain injuries, or internal damage. Do not remove your helmet at the scene unless trained medical personnel instruct you to, as helmet removal can worsen spinal injuries. Call 911 and accept emergency medical evaluation even if you feel you can walk it off. Adrenaline is a powerful pain masker, and delayed-onset injuries are extremely common in motorcycle crashes.
tips: Do not remove your own helmet — let paramedics handle this if a spinal injury is possible
Accept emergency medical evaluation even if you feel okay at the scene
Road rash and impact injuries can involve deeper tissue damage not visible externally

sections.heading: Document the Scene Thoroughly
sections.content: Photograph everything before your motorcycle is moved: the final position of all vehicles, skid marks, road conditions, debris, traffic signals, and signage. Photograph your protective gear — helmet, jacket, gloves — as damage to gear demonstrates the force of impact and your safety-conscious riding behavior. Get the license plates, insurance information, and driver's license details of all involved drivers. Gather witness contact information, as independent witnesses can counter any later claims of rider fault. Note whether the other driver admits to not seeing you, was on a phone, or ran a red light.
tips: Photograph your damaged helmet and gear — this is direct evidence of impact force
Note any admission by the other driver such as 'I didn't see you'
Witness testimony is especially valuable in motorcycle cases where rider-fault bias is common

sections.heading: Understanding Rider Bias — and How to Counter It
sections.content: Insurance adjusters and juries sometimes assume motorcyclists were speeding or riding aggressively — even when no evidence supports this. This bias can reduce settlement offers and damage jury outcomes. Counter it proactively: gather evidence of your sober, licensed, appropriately-geared riding. Your licensing and registration records, any dashcam or GoPro footage from your ride, and witness accounts of your speed and lane position all help build the factual record. Avoid posting about the accident on social media, where any photo or comment can be taken out of context.
tips: Collect any dashcam, GoPro, or traffic camera footage as soon as possible
Your safety gear shows responsible riding behavior — document it before discarding
Do not post about the crash on social media — anything you share can be used against you

sections.heading: Seek Medical Evaluation and Ongoing Care
sections.content: Motorcycle injuries frequently require specialist evaluation beyond initial emergency care. Orthopedic surgeons, neurologists, and physical therapists may all become part of your treatment team. Follow every referral and recommendation — gaps in treatment give insurers grounds to argue your injuries resolved or were not serious. Traumatic brain injuries from motorcycle crashes are often underdiagnosed initially; if you experience headaches, cognitive fog, mood changes, or sleep disruption in the days following the crash, report these symptoms to your doctor immediately and request a neurological evaluation.
tips: Report all symptoms including headache, dizziness, or memory issues after the crash
Attend every scheduled appointment and all specialist referrals
Keep a daily symptom journal, especially if you have a potential head injury

sections.heading: Know California and Arizona Helmet and Lane Splitting Laws
sections.content: In California, helmet use is mandatory for all motorcycle riders and passengers. Lane splitting — riding between lanes of slow or stopped traffic — is legal in California but must be done safely. In Arizona, helmet use is required for riders under 18; adults over 18 may ride without a helmet if they carry minimum insurance. Lane splitting is not legal in Arizona. If you were involved in lane splitting at the time of the crash in California, this does not automatically establish fault, but it may be a factor considered in the comparative fault analysis. Understanding how these laws apply to your situation affects how your claim is evaluated.
tips: California lane splitting is legal but 'safe and prudent' is a factual determination
Arizona prohibits lane splitting entirely — this may affect fault allocation
California helmet law: required for all riders regardless of age
